lines.text: A Nash equilibrium is a pair of strategies, one for each player, where neither player can improve their results by changing only their own strategy.
Think about what that really means: your opponent could know your exact strategy down to every frequency, and they still cannot beat you.
That is an extraordinary property, and it is what people mean when they say a strategy is unexploitable.
At equilibrium, any adjustment your opponent tries to make will not help them, because your strategy already accounts for it.
Equilibrium does not mean you will win every hand, or even most hands, it means no one can gain an edge over you through strategy alone.
John Nash proved that every finite game has at least one equilibrium, which means one always exists in poker, even if finding it is hard.
In two-player poker, the equilibrium strategies are often called GTO strategies, short for game-theory optimal.
Playing GTO guarantees you a certain minimum result: you will never be losing money to a player who is also playing GTO.
And against anyone who deviates from equilibrium, you have the opportunity to exploit them and do even better.
Equilibrium is your floor, not your ceiling, but it is a very solid floor to stand on.
